name: Typhoons Brian, Angela and Dan - Oct 1989
description:
Typhoon Brian
Typhoon Brian attained typhoon intensity on 2 October about 230km east of Yaxian, Hainan. It made landfall over Hainan about 30km east of Yaxian that evening. Brian passed just south of the city after midnight and soon entered open waters again. It then changed its track west-northwestwards and weakened to a severe tropical storm. Brian made landfall over the northern part of Viet Nam about 230km south of Hanoi on the afternoon of 3 October and weakened rapidly to a tropical storm. As it moved further inland, it further weakened to an area of low pressure in Laos.
Brian caused severe flooding in Hainan. About 96,000 hectares of paddy rice were inundated. Forty people were killed and 529 were injured. About 15,900 houses collapsed and 169,000 were damaged. Over 25 million lumber trees and rubber trees were blown down. There were about 1,300 reports of damage of dams and hydro-electric stations and 63 damage reports of bridges. Other damage included 134 boats, 700km of power cables and 2,100 km of telephone lines. The total damage was estimated to be about 837 million RMB. Off the coast of Hainan, a Hong Kong fishing vessel sank on the morning of 3 October. The area was searched but none of the seven crew members was found. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, October 1990)
Typhoon Angela
Typhoon Angela swept across the northern coast of Luzon during the early hours of 6 October. The flooding caused by Typhoon Angela left at least 118 people dead and 27 injured in northern Philippines. About 1,400 houses were destroyed, 3,300 houses were damaged, and 9,000 people were left homeless. The strong winds also toppled electric and telephone posts. Rice fields and tobacco land were inundated. Crops damaged were estimated to be about 1.6 million pesos. Angela entered the South China Sea on 6 October and weakened considerably after interacting with the terrain of northern Luzon. It continued its westward track across the northern part of the South China Sea at about 13 km/h. It weakened to a severe tropical storm on 10 October and made landfall over Viet Nam about 210 km northwest of Danang. Angela soon dissipated over land. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, October 1990)
Initial assessment of the damage in Viet Nam was as follows: 52 dead, 762 injured and 52 missing. At least 90,000 houses collapsed or were destroyed.  (UN DHA, 19 October 1989)
Typhoon Dan
Typhoon Dan swept across the central Philippines during the night of 10 October, and passed just south of Manila the following morning. According to press reports, 57 people were injured and 80 killed in the Philippines during the passage of Dan. Strong winds toppled trees and power pylons in Manila where power supply was cut off for more than 24 hours. The losses from damage to crops and property as well as industrial disruption ran to millions of US dollars.
Dan passed about 80 km south-southwest of Yaxian, Hainan early on 13 October. In Hainan, the city of Tongshi was severely flooded with water levels rising to 1.5 metres high. In Lingshui and Yaxian Counties, flooding occurred in several villages and caused some houses to collapse, leaving hundreds of people homeless. Two people were killed and 21 were injured. Damage to agriculture, power supply and communication was severe. The casualties due to Brian, Angela and Dan were reported to be 63 killed, 15 missing and 712 injured. About 30 million rubber trees were blown down. The total loss due to the three typhoons was estimated to be over 1,000 million RMB.
Dan weakened to a severe tropical storm on 13 October as it moved towards the Viet Nam coast. It made landfall about 300km south of Hanoi that evening. Dan finally dissipated in Laos about 180 km east-northeast of Vientiane early on 14 October.
In Viet Nam, 34 people were killed and 466 were injured. Floods caused by heavy rain swept away some 8,000 houses and several hundred boats. About 134,000 hectares of rice ready for harvest were inundated. Dan's powerful winds also blew down about 44,000 houses and tore off the roofs of another 500,000 houses. Traffic on the trans-Viet Namese highway was also blocked.  (Government of Hong Kong SAR, October 1990)

name: China: Earthquake - Oct 1989
description:
On 18 October 1989, an earthquake measuring 6.1 on the richter scale occurred in the area of Hebei, Shanxi provinces and inner Mongolia autonomous region, 220 km northwest of Beijing. According to verbal information from the Ministry of Civil Affairs, preliminary assessment of damages was as follows: 15 dead; 140 injured; 50,000 people made homeless; and 11,000 houses collapsed. (UN DHA, 20 Oct 1989)
On 31 October 1989, the Ministry of Civil Affairs updated the death toll to 29. (UN DHA, 31 Oct 1989)

name: Algeria: Earthquake - Oct 1989
description: On 29 October 1989, at 20:10 local time, a 6.0 richter scale earthquake hit northern Algeria. The epicenter was located in Oued Djer in the El Affroun region (60 km west of Algiers city), affecting the districts of Blida, Tipasa, Boumerdes, Wilaya and El-Nador. An aftershock of 4.0 hit the same area a few minutes later. Reported were 22 death, 184 injured, and serious damages in Wilaya Tipasa.OCHA, 30 Oct 1989

name: Typhoon Sarah - Sep 1989
description:
Typhoon Sarah was formed on 6 September. It moved westwards after developing into a tropical depression about 860 km north-northwest of Guam. On 11 September, Sarah made landfall over Taiwan about 190 km south of Taibei. It weakened to a severe tropical storm and moved along the east coast of Taiwan. Sarah entered the Taiwan Strait during the night of 12 September and continued to weaken as it approached the coast of eastern China. It made landfall over Zhejiang about 110 km south-southwest of Wenzhou the following afternoon and dissipated inland soon afterwards.
Rain associated with Sarah triggered off floods and landslides in the northern part of the Philippines, leaving 31 people dead. Over 200,000 people had to flee their homes. Rice and tobacco fields were ruined. Three bridges were destroyed by rampaging waters. The total damage in the Philippines was estimated to be at least 38 million pesos. In Taiwan, 19 people were killed while 14 others were reported missing. About 40 people were injured. Landslides and flooding triggered by heavy rain damaged bridges, roads and railways on the island. Electricity supply was interrupted and transportation was paralysed. Extensive agricultural areas were inundated. There were 28 houses destroyed and 41 houses damaged. The total loss in agriculture and forestry was estimated at about US$39 million. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, October 1989)

name: China: Typhoon Vera - Sep 1989
description:
On 17 September, Typhoon 'Vera' hit the coastal province of Zhejiang and caused floods which affected 37 counties and isolated over 700 villages. (UN DHA, 19 Sep 1989)
The Ministry of Civil Affairs reported that the typhoon also hit Jiangsu province where 34 persons died, 2,000 were injured, and 590,000 ha of farmland was affected. (UN DHA, 29 Sep 1989)

name: Myanmar: Fire - Aug 1989
description: On 27 July 1989, a fire broke out in the northwest township of Mandalay and was brought under control after 6 hours. One person was reportedly killed, 1,554 houses/buildings were destroyed, and 9,500 people from 1,980 household were rendered homeless. Total loss was estimated at US dollars 34 million. (UN DHA, 11 Aug 1989)

name: Indonesia: Earthquake - Aug 1989
description: On 1 August 1989, an earthquake measuring 5.6 on the richter scale occurred at 07:00 GMT (15:00 local time) in Irian Jaya district. The tremor caused a landslide of 200 m high and blocked Baliem river, and three villages upstream were inundated by water. 59 persons were reportedly dead, 15 were injured and 200 people were evacuated. The local authorities provided emergency assistance of food, clothing, blankets and cash grants. (UN DHA, 4 Aug 1989)

name: India: Floods - Aug 1989
description: Torrential monsoon rainstorms with winds of up to 75 km/h and floods from 23 to 31 July affected some coastal areas. Damage to crops, cattle heads, and immovable property was estimated at several million rupees. The death toll rose to over 800 in Maharashtra, 144 in Andhra Pradesh, 24 in Gujarat, and 73 in other districts. About 550 people were missing in Maharashtra. (UN DHA, 3 Aug 1989)

name: Bangladesh: Floods - Aug 1989
description: Following excessive rainfall in neighboring countries, flash floods occurred in the Sylhet district. The affected Upazilas are Zakiganj, Kanaighat, Bianibazar, and Golapgang. The most affected was Zakiganj where a break in the embankment caused a rush of water from the Kushiara river.  200,000 people in the above-mentioned Upazilas were affected. There were also reportedly flash floods in Bandarban district, and a tidal surge in Cox's Bazar and Chittagong districts. (UN DHA, 14 Aug 1989)

name: Viet Nam: Typhoon Irving - Aug 1989
description: Tropical Cyclone Irving formed over the South China Sea about 350km west of Manila early on 21 July. Irving intensified into a severe tropical storm early on 23 July and turned to the northwest, moving almost parallel to the Vietnam coast. Despite weakening to a tropical storm that afternoon, Irving temporarily re-gained severe tropical storm strength before making landfall about 200km south of Hanoi next morning. It finally dissipated inland over Laos about 160km south-west of Hanoi on the afternoon of 24 July. In Vietnam, 102 people died and 488 were injured. Over 80,000 houses were damaged. About 65,000 hectares of farmland were inundated and 256 boats sank. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, October 1990

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Jul 1989
description:
Typhoon Gordon hit and severely affected the northern Philippines island of Luzon on Saturday 15 July, causing floods and landslides in the northern and central regions of the island. A bridge in Ilocos Norte washed away and a dike in la union Province collapsed flooding three villages. Landslides and debris blocked roads. (UN DHA, 18 Jul 1989)
Typhoon Gordon, the most powerful storm to hit the Philippines to date during 1989, causing floods and landslides along with massive infrastructural damage, was finally officially assessed as causing the following damage and loss of life: total deaths of 94, injured 382, and missing 3. Over 95,000 families were affected (representing approximately 486,000 people) with 8,845 families actually rendered homeless. (UN DHA, 15 Aug 1989)

name: China: Floods and Landslides - Jul 1989
description:
Heavy rains poured on Sichuan province from 7 to 10 July. The  maximum rainfall recorded was 500 mm in Wushen county. Floods and landslides occurred in several areas. Chongqing city in Sichuan was the most seriously affected. In Huaying city of Sichuan province, a landslide with 1 million cubic meters at 1,000 meter long and 600 meter high engulfed 2 villages and 6 working units. (UN DHA, 27 July 1989)
The latest data is as follows: 2,000 people died; more than 10,000 were injured; and 100 million were affected (of which 60 million seriously); 1.2 million houses collapsed and 2.78 million were damaged; and more than 8.5 million ha of farmland was damaged. (UN DHA, 6 Sep 1989)

name: Sri Lanka: Floods - Jun 1989
description:
The UNDP/UNDRO resident representative reports heavy rains during the past several days which caused floods and landslides in the southwestern part of Sri Lanka. The worst affected area was Bulathkohupitiya in the Kegalle district. Other affected districts were Eliya, Gampaha, Galle, Kalutara, Matara, Nuwara and Ratnapura. (UN DHA, 6 Jun 1989)
According to the UNDP/UNDRP resident representative, the latest official estimates show more than 300 dead and 300,000 homeless. (UN DHA, 8 Jun 1989)
With receding flood waters, victims in the Colombo area who were previously housed in temples and schools are preparing to return to their houses. The number of affected persons in the most affected districts (Colombo, Galle, Gampaha, Kalutara, Kegalle, Ratnapura) is now estimated at 250,000. (UN DHA, 9 Jun 1989)

name: Viet Nam: Cyclone Cecil - May 1989
description:
Typhoon Cecil formed as a tropical depression over the central part of the South China Sea about 740 km east-southeast of Danang on the evening of 22 May. On May 25 it made landfall about 80 km southeast of Danang and turned northwestwards again. Cecil weakened rapidly and became a tropical storm when it was about 110 km northwest of Danang. It moved westwards into Laos and weakened into an area of low pressure about 210 km west northwest of Danang on the evening of 25 May.
Cecil brought torrential rain to the central part of Vietnam and caused catastrophic floods there. In the city of Hue, streets were under two metres of water. In the central provinces of Vietnam, 140 people were killed and about 600 were reported missing. About 112,000 hectares of rice paddy and 40,000 hectares of other crops were destroyed or damaged. In addition, about 36,000 houses and 150 schools were destroyed, leaving 150,000 people homeless. Fishing boats which sank or were damaged totalled 700. There were also reports of waterworks and dams being destroyed. The remnants of Cecil also brought heavy rain to the central and northeastern parts of Thailand. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, October 1990)

name: China: Hailstorm - Apr 1989
description:
On 20 April, a hailstorm with winds of up to 28 meters per second swept across several counties in Sichuan province for over 5 hours. The most affected areas were Fushun, Longchang, Yongchuan, Jinyan and Gulin counties including cities of Chongqing, Leshan, Luzhou, Neijiang and Nigong. (UN DHA, 28 Apr 1989)
In the south-east part of Sichuan province, in the west part of which people suffered from a series of earthquakes, they were also affected by severe hailstorms since the end of April. The most affected towns were Luzhou and Zigong. Damage was reported as follows: 157 dead; more than 30 million people in 105 counties affected; 19 million ha of crops stricken; 790,000 mt of grain lost; schools and factories were forced to close their operations. (UN DHA, 19 May 1989)

name: Bangladesh: Tornado - Apr 1989
description: A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reported updated information on a tornado that struck Manikganj district on 26 April as follows: the most affected sub-districts were Saturia, Daulatpur, and Manikgank Sadar; 522 were confirmed dead; the final toll may reach 1,000; 10,000 families were affected, and there was almost a total destruction of buildings within a 6 km radius from Saturia. (UN DHA, 1 May 1989)

name: China: Earthquake - Apr 1989
description: On 16 April, at 5:34 local time, an earthquake measuring 6.7 on the richter scale occurred in Batang county of Sichuan province.  Among 10,000 aftershocks over 1 richter scale were recorded, major aftershocks hit the same area on 25 April with 6.7 magnitude, on 3 May with 6.4 and on 4 May with 6.2 in Batang county of Sichuan province. As of 10 May, damages were reported as follows:  8 dead, 107 injured and over 9,000 houses in 23 townships collapsed/damaged. A WFP field mission to disaster-stricken areas found the worst affected town was Zhubalong and almost all of the houses in Batang were flattened and a major bridge collapsed. The Government has already provided 230 mt of barley and evacuated 40,000 people, who are temporarily accomodated in makeshift camps of plastic sheets and sacking. The Government of China welcomes any appropriate emergency assistance from friendly governments and international organizations. (UN DHA, 18 May 1989)

name: Myanmar: Fire - Feb 1989
description:
On 16 February 1989,a major fire broke out in Mergui southern Burma and took eight hours to bring under control. Buildings destroyed include 3 schools, 5 fire stations, 3 cinema halls and road transport administration department building. Total loss was estimated at US dollars 48 million (UN DHA, 24 Feb 1989)
A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reported updated damage as follows: 1,792 houses destroyed; 4,242 households affected; and 22,324 people rendered homeless. (UN DHA, 28 Mar 1989)

name: Philippines: Floods - Feb 1989
description:
Following continuous rain due to a low-pressure zone for the past 6 weeks, areas of Sorsogon, Camarines Sur and Samar have been hit by flash floods.  (UN DHA, 6 Feb 1989)
The Office of Civil Defence updated the damage as follows: 39 dead, 11 injured, 5 missing; over 260,000 people affected; more than 2,500 houses totally/partially destroyed; and total damage estimated at approximately US dollars 6 million (UN DHA, 9 Feb 1989)

name: USSR - Tajikistan: Earthquake - Jan 1989
description:
An earthquake of magnitude 7 of the 12-point Soviet scale (Richetr magnitude 5.9) occurred in the Soviet Republic of Tajikistan on Monday, 23 January 1989, at 02:02 hours A.M local time (23:02 GMT). The epicenter was located in the village of Sharor on the Soviet-Afghan border, some 50km southwest of the republic’s capital Dushanbe. While the earthquake affected mainly mud houses, the greatest material damage and human loss seem to have been caused by a subsequent 2km long landslide. (UN DHA, 23 January 1989)
The United Nations representative in Moscow reported that the villages of Sharora, Okulibolo and Okulipoyen were destroyed by the mud landslide. Roads and communication lines were disrupted and thousands of heads of livestock were buried in the mud. (UN DHA, 24 January 1989)
The total number of dead reached 274 people, including 100 children. In Sharora, 207 were reported dead, and in Okulibolo 67. The population in Okulipoen had time to escape. (UN DHA, 27 January 1989)

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Nov 1988
description:
On 7 November, typhoon 'Skip' with center winds up to 175 km/h hit the Philippines and triggered landslides and floods. The most affected areas were the national capital, southern Tagalog, Bicol, and Western Visayas which have not yet recovered from damage by typhoon Ruby. (UN DHA, 9 Nov 1988)
Typhoon 'Skip' (Yoning) caused 128 deaths, 29 injured people, and 161 missing people, according to the UNDRP/UNDP resident representative. Over one million people were reportedly affected, among whom 45,000 were homeless. 25,000 houses were totally/partially damaged. Damages to crops/infrastructure/properties were estimated at US dollars 32 million. The island of Samar was the most severely affected. (UN DHA, 11 Nov 1988)

name: China: Earthquake - Nov 1988
description: On 6 November 1988, an earthquake measuring 7.6 on the richter scale hit southwest China in Lancang and Menglian counties of Yunnan province. The population of 14 counties in Yunnan province is 3.1 million, of which over 1 million people were affected by the earthquake. 3,364 persons were seriously injured. Over 200,000 buildings, including 144,000 houses, collapsed. In addition, 500,000 buildings, including 253,000 houses, were damaged. Almost 1,000 schools and 98 clinics were destroyed. In Lancang county, 2,590 persons were injured, and 70 percent of the population was affected. 74 percent of houses were damaged/destroyed and 40,000 mt of grain was reportedly ruined. (UN DHA, 14 Nov 1988)

name: Thailand: Floods and Mudslides - Nov 1988
description: Torrential monsoon rains triggered major mudslides in the region of Nakhon Si Thammarat province in the South of Thailand. The government indicated that there were 334 dead, 1,878 injured and 330 missing amongst a total of 978,232 affected (including 43,349 displaced). Material damage included 51,250 houses, 3,802 roads, 833 bridges, 144 dykes and 618 schools. (UN DHA, 27 November 1988)

name: Bangladesh: Cyclone - Nov 1988
description:
A cyclone with wind speeds above 150 km per hour crossed inland over the Patuakhali/Khulua coast during the night on 29 November 1988 ending in a cyclonic storm over Madaripur district, south of Dhaka. (UN DHA, 30 Nov 1988)
A UNDRO/UNDP Res rep reported that the most affected areas were Bagherhat, Barguna, Bhola, Jessore, Khulna, Patuakahli, Pirozpur, Satkhira and Sundarbans.  (UN DHA, 1 Dec 1988)
The cyclone left a trail of devastation: human corpses, dead livestock, and wildlife were found scattered. The death toll was over 2,000, showing an upward trend with many fishermen still missing. Other official figures indicated crop damage in all affected districts on 160,000 hectares. Losses were estimated at 200,000 tons. Estimates of homeless were 2.5 million. (UN DHA, 12 Dec 1988)

name: Typhoon Ruby - Oct 1988
description: Typhoon Ruby made landfall over central Luzon early on 25 October and entered the South China Sea later that morning, passing about 70 km to the south-southwest of Baguio. The death toll on land was over 130 and financial losses totalled over US$50 million. Ruby weakened into a severe tropical storm on 27 October and degenerated further into a tropical storm early on 28 October under the influence of strengthening northeast monsoon. That afternoon, it swept across Hainan. Two people were killed and about 133 million RMB of damage was incurred. Ruby finally weakened into an area of low pressure over the sea areas to the southwest of the island on 29 October. During the passage of Ruby over the South China Sea, high seas associated with the outer circulation of Ruby affected the coast of Fujian Province and caused financial damage of about 37 million RMB. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, September 1989

name: India: Floods - Oct 1988
description: Continuous torrential rains in the hills since 21 September caused heavy floods in northwestern India. Vast areas in Punjab, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, and Delhi were inundated causing widespread loss of life, damage to crops/property, and paralysing surface and air traffic. The media reported that the death toll reached 174 in four affected states.  In Haryana, Yamuna river has flooded 25 villages in Jagadhri subdivision. In Delhi, the river Yamuna was flowing well above the danger mark and has flooded a number of colonies and transportation networks close to the river from where more than 16,000 people were evacuated to temporary camps. (UN DHA, 4 Oct 1988)

name: India: Floods - Aug 1988
description: Monsoon rains caused flooding in Brahmaputra and Ganges and their tributaries resulting in a disaster situation in north-eastern India. The States of Uttar Pradesh, Assam, Bihar, west Bengal and Arunachal Pradesh were affected. According to press reports, flooding claimed more than 850 lives. In Uttar Pradesh alone, 453 deaths were officially reported. 12,373 villages and 1.7 million hectares of land were affected.  In Assam, the army rescued 15,000 people from a flood-hit area in Gauhati on 29/30 August. The surging Brahmaputra and other rivers have affected 5.6 million people. The Assam death toll increased to 46. Displaced people were accommodated in 25 relief camps. In Bihar state, swollen Ganges and Kosi flooded 100 villages while Bagmati and Adhwara rivers flooded vast areas. 14 deaths were reported from West Bengal. (UN DHA, 31 Aug 1988)

name: India: Earthquake - Aug 1988
description:
A strong earthquake measuring 6.7 on the richter scale struck India's Bihar state and eastern Nepal, at 4.39 am (Indian Standard Time) on 21 august 1988 (corresponding to 23.09 hrs GMT, 20 august 1988). The epicentre was located 80 kms north of Dharbhanga, Bihar state, on the Indo-Nepal border. The quake and aftershocks were also felt in the north eastern Indian states of Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh and West Bengal as well as Sikkim and Bangladesh. it was the strongest quake in the sub-himalayan terrain on the Indo-Nepal border since the one in 1934. (UN DHA, 22 Aug 1988)
Several aftershocks were felt in Bihar since the August 21 earthquake which did not cause additional damage or injuries. The confirmed official death toll rose to more than 200 as of 24 august 1988. Unofficial sources still put figure considerably higher. (UN DHA, 30 Aug 1988)
It is estimated that out of a population of 7.7 million in the three districts severely affected by the quake, some 30,000 families require emergency assistance through the provision of rice, clothing, utensils, building materials and other relief goods available in country. The Indian Red Cross is establishing a relief assistance programme for 5,000 families. (UN DHA, 30 Aug 1988)

name: Nepal: Earthquake - Aug 1988
description:
A strong earthquake, measuring 6.7 on the richter scale, occurred on 20 August at 23.09 GMT near the Nepal/India border about 160 kms south-east of Kathmandu. The first reports from Nepal indicated that the worst affected areas were Daharan/Dhankuta/Terathum/Panchthar/Ilam/Morang and Bhaktapur in the eastern part of Kathmandu Valley. (UN DHA, 22 August 1988)
WHO/Ministry of Health estimated that 60,000 persons were affected in the eastern region. (UN DHA, 23 August 1988). Field reports indicated that nearly 18,000 houses/public buildings were destroyed or unsafe and needed to be demolished. (UN DHA, 29 August 1988). The Home Ministry confirmed that 708 people died and 1,013 were injured, of which 535 were serious. (UN DHA, 1 September 1988)

name: China: Typhoon No.7 - Aug 1988
description:
A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reports that a typhoon hit Xiangshan county, whirling through Ninbo, Shaoxin, Hangzhou and Huzhou, and the force of the typhoon decreased and disappeared while crossing Anhui province. The typhoon affected 41 counties. Electrical power, telecommunications, highways and channels were seriously damaged, villagers were trapped, houses toppled over and crops were waterlogged. Most sanitary and civil facilities were damaged. Very high losses were registered in Hainin, Fenghua, and Chengxian as these counties had previously been affected by floods.
Reports indicate that: 10.5 million people in a 15,000 sq.km. range were affected; 135,000 people were evacuated; 160 died; 34 were missing; and 1,232 were injured.  (UN DHA, 17 Aug 1988)

description:
Following heavy rains and floods in the last two weeks of August: 125 people died; 13 million people were affected; 35 districts out of 64, areas of 25,000 sq.km. were affected; 370,000 ha of crop areas were fully and 391,000 ha were partially damaged. (UN DHA, 30 Aug 1988)
The official death toll is now over 500, many due to snakebites, drinking contaminated water, and consuming rotten foodstuffs. (UN DHA, 5 Sep 1988)

name: China: Floods in Zhejiang Province - Aug 1988
description: On 29 - 30 July, heavy rains caused violent floods in Zhejiang province, southeast China. In 12 hours rainfall reached 350-400 mm. 11 counties and cities were affected. The hardest hit was Ningbo city with 256 dead, 347 missing, and 200 injured, 120,000 people were stranded in an inaccessible area. 30,000 houses collapsed. 73,000 ha of farmland was flooded including 28,000 ha destroyed. Water in the affected area was contaminated and poisoned by rotting corpses and farm chemicals. Drinking water was in great shortage and freshwater from other places was not sufficient. (UN DHA, 4 Aug 1988)

So far 61 people were reported dead with damages to standing crops estimated at over 120,000 ha. (UN DHA, 12 Jul 1988)

description: In June 1988, major floods affected provinces of Badakhshan, Herat and Jouzjan in addition to Kabul, Takhar, Samangan, Baghlan. The government reports that 3,457 houses were totally and 1,570 partially devastated, and an estimated 40,000 people, thousand of hectares of cropland, irrigation system and hundreds of km of roads were affected. Total damages estimated by the government were US$260 million. The government has asked UNDRO to launch an appeal for international assistance. The government identified needs for 161,000 victims, including 32,000 children (UN DHA, 27 July 1988)

name: China: Floods - May 1988
description:
In Fujian province (in particular in counties Jianyang, Chongan, Zhenge), heavy rains caused floods since 20 May.  The rain continued as of 24 May with rainfall recorded up to 257 millimeters on 21 May. (UN DHA, 24 May 1988)
Updated information indicates a steep rise in the population affected: 91 deaths, 657 injured, 1 missing, 100,000 people in unreachable areas, and 100,000 displaced. 6,000 houses collapsed and there was damage to 3,908 kilometers of roads, 715 bridges, and 5,937 units of water supply system. The total loss was estimated to be up to 72 million US dollars. (UN DHA, 27 May 1988)

name: Myanmar: Fire - Mar 1988
description: The northeastern town of Iasho, Shan state, 1,200 kilometres from Rangoon, was devastated by fires on 20 March 1988. First reports indicate the fire has destroyed 2,096 houses, two primary schools and killed 113 persons. More than 20,000 persons were rendered homeless, equivalent to 20 per cent of the population.  (UN DHA, 24 Mar 1988)

name: Pacific Islands: Tropical Cyclone - Jan 1988
description:
Tropical cyclone Anne, 700 miles west ofFiji, is heading southwest at 15 knots towards New Caledonia with winds of 100 knots gusting to 140 knots.
InVanuatu, the hurricane alert was lifted. No damage has so far been reported to the northern islands. In theSolomon Islands,  the cyclone passed close to the eastern islands with some damage reported, but so far no assessment figures are available apart from 2000 people estimated as affected. (UN DHA, 12 Jan 1988)
In theMarshall Islands(typhoon Ebeye), Kwajalein atol marshall was hit by the typhoon with minor damage to 500 houses and 350 totally, rendering 3500 people homeless.  (UN DHA, 12 Jan 1988)
In theSolomon islands, the UNDRO/UNDP resident representative in Fiji reported that the cyclone Anne damage to the Solomon Islands was minimal: 25 houses, and 5 classrooms in Anuta, Duffs, and Manuopo. (UN DHA, 15 Jan 1988)
InVanuatu, the total number of people rendered homeless is 3,100 of which 1,600 are the most severely affected. (UN DHA, 20 Jan 1988)
Tropical cyclone Anne affectedNew Caledonia, principally on the east coast and in the south where crop damage has been reported, but it has not been declared a disaster area. However the crop damage has been estimated at between 80 and 100 percent. (UN DHA, 20 Jan 1988)

name: Indonesia: Earthquake - Dec 1987
description: On the morning of 26 November 1987, an earthquake (magnitude 5.8 richter) hit south Pantar Island in eastern Indonesia and caused a landslide which cut access/escape routes from villages to the sea. 43 people died, 101 were injured, 75 were missing and 16,000 people in ten villages were affected. 1,126 buildings were damaged, including mosques, schools, government. buildings and village dwellings. 1,350 villagers were evacuated to a nearby island. (UN DHA, 2 Dec 1987)

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Nov 1987
description:
Typhoon Sisang codename Nina with a strength of 250 kph hit the coastal central Philippines on 25 November 1987. The typhoon touched 19 provinces and created great damage to properties, infrastructure, communications, and the population.  (UN DHA, 28 Nov 1987)
According to recent reports, there are 656 dead, 390 injured and 152 still missing. The number of affected families has risen to 219,218 and homeless families to 40,174. Damage to public and private property is now estimated at (Pesos 515,171,935) US dollars 25,266,000. The number of houses totally destroyed was 90,173 and partially destroyed was 109,663. (UN DHA, 9 Dec 1987)

name: Micronesia: Tropical Storm - Nov 1987
description: The UNDRO/UNDP resident representative has reported that during the early hours of the 21st of November, Truk was devastated by tropical storm Nina with winds exceeding 80 knots. To date,  there have been four confirmed deaths, one missing person, three critically injured persons, and scores of minor injuries. Hundreds are homeless and temporarily sheltered in undamaged public buildings. About 95 percent of crops were damaged or destroyed. (UN DHA, 27 Nov 1987)

name: Indonesia: Floods - Nov 1987
description: Heavy monsoon rains have caused floods and landslides in Aceh province, northern Sumatra. Official reports indicate serious losses in crops, unhusked rice, and livestock. Damages were reported as follows: important road cuts in Central province, many houses either destroyed or seriously damaged, 4 persons killed by landslides, and several thousand persons were evacuated. (UN DHA, 4 Nov 1987)

name: Viet Nam: Typhoon Maury - Nov 1987
description:
On 11 November, Typhoon Maury formed as a tropical depression about 1,550 km east of Manila and moved west-northwestwards. It swept across the southeastern part of Luzon on 14 November and passed about 200 km south of Manila. Maury entered the South China Sea on the night of 15 November and intensified to a tropical storm on 17 November. However, it weakened into a tropical depression on 19 November and landed over southern Viet Nam about 350 km south-southeast of Danang in the early afternoon. It dissipated rather rapidly soon afterwards. Heavy rain associated with Maury caused widespread damage in Viet Nam. At least 86 people were killed and more than 56,000 houses and 15 bridges were damaged. Crops and livestock were also severely affected. The total loss was estimated at more than US$20 million. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, May 1988)
Typhoon Maury inflicted heavy damage on the provinces of Nghia Binh and Phu Khanh. The typhoon was the most recent in a series of disasters affecting Viet Nam during 1987 which included storms, droughts and outbreaks of crop diseases that severely cut agricultural output by an estimated 1.7 million tons of foodgrain. Figures received from UNDRO/UNDP resident representative on the direct damage caused by Typhoon Maury are as follows: 101 dead, 211 badly injured, 352,000 homeless (or whose property and food supplies have been destroyed), and a further 584,800 whose homes, property and food supplies were severely damaged. The estimated shortfall on food losses from Typhoon Maury alone was 65,500 tons. (UN DHA, 29 December, 1987)

name: Typhoon Betty - Aug 1987
description:
On 13 August, Typhoon Betty slammed into the Philippines causing heavy damage and casualties in several regions: Batangas, Laguna, Bicol, Mindoro, Cavite, and Luzon. The latest information provided by the UNDRO/UNDP representative was: nine dead; two injured; six people missing, more than 3,000 families affected; more than 600 persons homeless; 343 houses totally destroyed; and 407 houses damaged. (UN DHA, 14 Aug 1987)
The UNDRP Resrep reported that on 16 August 1987 Typhoon Betty struck the provinces of Binh Tri Thien and Nghe Tinh causing loss of lives, damage to infrastructure, and agriculture. The following are estimated figures regarding the total overall damage: nine dead; 241 injured; 14,583 houses collapsed; and 165,505 without roofs. (UN DHA, 2 Sep 1987)

name: Nepal: Floods - Aug 1987
description: Following torrential rain, for the past two weeks, extensive flood and landslide damage has been reported throughout the country. Particularly affected are central and eastern Terai districts and Central Hills. In addition to the damage to crops, houses, public infrastructure (mainly transportation), and private property, many families were rendered homeless. (UN DHA, 14 Aug 1987)

name: China: Storms/Mud Rock Flow - Aug 1987
description: On 15, 18, and 19 August, heavy storms affected numerous counties in Dandong city and Dalian city in the Liaoning province in north-east China causing mountain torrents, river flooding and mud-rock flows over a wide area. The storm centre was located in Daguanzhi town in Xiuyan county and the total rainfall was between 300 and 450 mm. 63 persons died, 38 were reported missing and 860 were injured. 13,000 houses were severely damaged and approximately 10,000 families were affected. 60,000 ha were affected (32,000 seriously) involving 80 counties and 676 villages. (UN DHA, 28 Aug 1987)

name: Rep. of Korea: Typhoon/Flood/Landslides - Jul 1987
description: Since 16 July, two devastating catastrophes have hit the Republic of Korea, namely Typhoon Thelma, and the heavy storms resulting in floods and landslides. Typhoon Thelma, which swept across the southern coast with winds reaching 130 kph, caused enormous devastation. More than 300 people were reported dead or missing and over 20,000 were made homeless. On 22/23 July, torrential non-stop rain fell in Seoul and the central area of Korea for a period of 24 hours (July 22/23) causing widespread havoc and destruction. The Kum river flooded, and the hardest-hit areas were chungchong-nam, Kwangwon, and Kyonggi provinces. Nearly 100 people were listed as dead with 38 missing. Twenty-six thousand people were made homeless and 54,000 hectares of cultivated land were flooded. Houses, roads, and the central infrastructure were again seriously damaged. (UN DHA, 29 Jul 1987)

name: Bangladesh: Cyclonic Storm/Tidal Surge/Floods - Jun 1987
description:
On 5 June at midnight, a deep depression (previously formed in the Bay of Bengal) crossed the Hatiya, Swandwip, Patuakhali, and Bhola regions as a cyclonic storm accompanied by a tidal wave of up to 6 feet above normal and inundated low-lying islands. Heavy rains in the entire southern region caused flash floods in a few areas. (UN DHA, 10 Jun 1987)
The overall flood situation has seriously deteriorated following heavy rains on the 30th and 31st of july and the rush of floodwater from the upper region. The worst affected districts were Rangpur, Netrokona, Gaibandha, Noagaon, Kurigram, Jamalpur, Cox's Bazar, Chittagong, and Noakhali. About 3.5 million people were reportedly affected and approximately 650,000 acres of crops were considerably damaged. (UN DHA, 3 Aug 1987)
The number of districts affected was 50; the number of Upazilas/municipalities affected was 335/17; 939,557 houses were fully damaged; 790,762 houses were partially damaged, and total deaths were reported to be 570. (UN DHA, 7 Sep 1987)

name: Indonesia: Heavy Rains/Floods - May 1987
description: A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reported heavy rains in Bengkulu province, southern Sumatra, which caused a 3-4 meter rise in the river level on 10 May 1987. Consequently, flooding destroyed about 126 houses, 37 people were reported drowned, 8 were found dead, and others were missing.  (UN DHA, 19 May 1987)

name: China: Floods - May 1987
description: Serious flooding has occurred in Guangdong province in southern China. Up to 70 deaths have been reported, and more than 10,000 hectares of farmland involving 31 villages were reported to be under water in the worst affected area, Zhangpu County. An assessment indicated the possibility of 158,000 hectares of farmland swamped and 5,300 houses washed away. 350,000 people were evacuated to safe areas. Guangzhou city reported that these were the worst floods since 1974 and 4 major rivers in provinces were flowing at two metres above the danger level. (UN DHA, 26 May 1987)

name: China: Forest Fire - May 1987
description: A UNDRO/UNDP representative reports that a major fire broke out on 6 May in Gulian forest, Mohe county of Heilongjiang province, killing 142 people, rendering 51,000 homeless. More than 300,000 hectares of forest was affected. Over 27,000 inhabitants of Xilinji and Tuqiang  were evacuated. (UN DHA, 13 May 1987). As of 14 May 1987, the fire has so far killed 168, seriously injured 300 and made 40,000 homeless. A total of 11,700 families have been affected.  (UN DHA, 14 May 1987)

name: Vanuatu: Cyclone Uma - Feb 1987
description:
Cyclone Uma hit Vanuatu during the night of 7 to 8 February 1987. Eight hours of 100-knot winds left the capital Port Vila without water, electricity or communications services. The capital was declared a disaster area with heavy damage to buildings and housing. (UN DHA, 9 Feb 1987)
Relief and recovery activities have started in the capital. The government established evacuation centres for some 5,000 people who were made homeless. (UN DHA, 10 Feb 1987)
The confirmed death toll was 45 with major economic and infrastructural losses estimated at  $US150 million. (UN DHA, 11 Feb 1987)

name: Papua New Guinea: Earthquake - Feb 1987
description:
A series of small tremors preceded a severe earthquake (7.4 richter scale) at 04.34 hours on 9 February 1987 which affected Umboi and outlying islands in Vitiaz strait, as well as outer reaches Morobe and west New Britain provinces. The epicentre was under Umboi Island, about 440 km northeast of Port Moresby. A few deaths/injuries have been reported so far. 12,000 people were affected on Umboi Island and several thousand on outlying islands. (UN DHA, 10 Feb 1987)
Morobe provincial government has declared Unboi Island a disaster area. Further tremors were reported from Semo (Siassi district) on 12 February. The homeless was estimated at 2,500 (500 families). Additional damage was reported to buildings and roads. The Government requested emergency assistance from Australia (jerrycans and tents/tarpaulins). (UN DHA, 13 Feb 1987)

name: Algeria: Earthquakes - Jan 1987
description:
On 26-27 January 1987, earthquakes struck a hilly/woody area 200 km west of Algiers, leaving 1 dead, 7 injured, and 900 homeless.  Reported were 3 tremors ranging 3.3-4.8 on the richter scale but no damage.
The series of quakes occurred in Wilaya de Tissemsilt, Daira de Bordj Bou Naama, and the village of Larbaa (30 km from Chlef).OCHA, 28 Jan 1987

name: Western Samoa/Cook Islands: Cyclone Sally - Jan 1987
description:
A UNDRP/UNDP representative reports that cyclone Sally was the second to attack the South Pacific in the last few days (first was Rajah), and hit the Cook Islands on 1 Jan 1987. Heavy rains, rough seas, and wind gusts affected mostly island Rarotonga, where the population was evacuated. (UN DHA, 6 Jan 1987)
Cyclone Sally was one of the worst natural disasters ever to hit the Cook Islands with winds of up to 190 km/hour and waves of up to 12 metres high. To date, no deaths and only five injuries were reported. Early warnings allowed persons to evacuate the low-lying areas in time. On Rarotonga island (population 9,000) approximately 1,000 persons were in shelters and 5,000 more were staying with relatives/friends. In Avarua (capital of Rarotonga), 80 percent of buidings were destroyed or severely damaged. The harbour was completely blocked, and the water supply was disrupted in half of the town. (UN DHA, 8 Jan 1987)

name: Fiji: Cyclone Rajah - Dec 1986
description:
Cyclone Rajah hit north Vanua Levu on 29/30 December 1986, moving through Lau group toward Tonga. Considerable damage to crops, roads, bridges, and water/power supply system was reported in Vanua Levu, Taveuni and nearby islands. (UN DHA, 31 Dec 1986)
The areas most affected were east Vanua Levu, Taveuni and Lau groups. One person was missing at sea and one was injured. About 260,000 persons were reported in affected areas. Forty-one emergency centres with food distribution were opened for over 3,000 evacuees.  (UN DHA, 31 Dec 1986)

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Oct 1986
description:
The UNDRO/UNDP representative reported that Typhoon Oyang hit the Philippines at 0400 am on 6 Oct 1986. (UN DHA, 7 Oct 1986)
The UNDRO/UNDP representative provided the following update: 11 dead, 26 persons injured, 11 persons missing; and 14,115 families or 82,529 persons in metro Manila affected and evacuated. The estimated cost of damages to roads, bridges and public works was approximately US dollars 1,200,000. (UN DHA, 8 Oct 1986)

name: Bangladesh: Floods - Sep 1986
description:
A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reported that serious flooding was caused by incessant rain during the last four days throughout the country. The south-western districts of Khulna, Bagerhat, Sathkhira, Pathuakhali, and Barguna were reported to be seriously affected areas. (UN DHA, 30 Sep 1986)
Approximately 3.4 million people were affected by flooding in 2,890 sq. miles in 25 districts. 30 total deaths were reported, mostly due to drowning or injuries from collapsed houses. Crops were damaged in approximately 1.3 million acres, mainly in the greater districts of Khulna, Jessore, and Faridpur. (UN DHA, 10 Oct 1986)

name: Typhoon Vera - Aug 1986
description:
Typhoon Vera developed about 1,020 km east-northeast of Manila on 15 August. (Gov’t of Hong Kong, 1987) It struck China on 27 August, resulting in heavy casualties and property loss. In Shanghai, eight people died and 25 were injured, and 500 houses were destroyed. (UN DHA, 29 August 1986)
The typhoon then hit South Korea, causing US$10.9 million of damage. At least 33 people were dead or missing, 73 were injured, and 6,623 were made homeless. At least 1,852 houses and 929 fishing boats were also damaged. (UN DHA, 03 September 1986)

name: Typhoon Wayne - Aug 1986
description:
Philippines
Typhoon Medinh (Wayne) struck the Philippines on 2 September causing 10 deaths. Around 65 houses were totally damaged. There was also significant damage to agriculture and infrastructure. The typhoon flooded 70 percent of Metro Manila and Luzon, stranding thousands of people. Stricken areas included Pangasinan, Bataan, Bulcan, Pampanga, Benguet, Abra, Zambales and Metro Manila. (UN DHA, 5 September 1986)
The UNDRO/UNDP representative later reported that 23 people died and 70,113 families were  affected. There were 438 totally and 555 partially damaged houses, and the total damage to agriculture and infrastructure was calculated at over US$6 million. (UN DHA, 8 September 1986)
Viet Nam
Typhoon Wayne struck Viet Nam from 1 to 3 a.m. on 6 September with winds reaching 120km/hour. (UN DHA, 13 September 1986) The typhoon resulted in 401 dead, 34 missing, and 2,502 injured - including 921 children and 894 women. Several thousand houses were destroyed or damaged as well as schools, hospitals, nurseries and warehouses. Around 13,930 hectares of rice crop were affected and 82,650 hectares flooded. (UN DHA, 25 September 1986)

name: Bangladesh: Floods - Aug 1986
description: A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative in Dhaka reported that floods were caused by monsoon rains in the Ganges basin in the north-western Bangladesh district of Rajshahi. The water rose sharply in the last two weeks and flooded 1600 sq miles affecting 100,000 people. Four persons were reportedly killed and 48,500 acres of crops were damaged. (UN DHA, 11 Aug 1986)

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Jul 1986
description:
The UNDRO/UNDP representative in Manila reports that typhoon Gading (codename Peggy) hit regions i (Ilocos) iii (Central Luzon), iv (Southern Tagalos) and metro Manila on 9 July 1986. The National Weather Service, U.S.A., reports that the typhoon reached southern China. (UN DHA, 11 Jul 1986)
The UNDRP/UNDP representative in Manila reports a further increase in casualties and damage from typhoon Gading (Peggy). As of 17 July: 89 dead, 20 missing, 16 injured; 119,386 families (644,608 persons) affected; and 6,679 houses partially damaged. (UN DHA, 18 Jul 1986)

name: China: Typhoons/Floods - Jul 1986
description:
A UNDRP/UNDP representative in Beijing reports that two disasters hit China almost simultaneously. Typhoon Peggy struck the south China coast and a strong tornado hit the east coast. In Shanghai, a powerful tornado, the worst in 30 years, lashed the southeastern suburbs. (UN DHA, 15 Jul 1986)
A UNDRO/UNDP representative provided the following update: 172 dead; 800 injured; over 510,000 people stranded by floods; 55 cities severely hit by rainfall averaging 200 mm and in some places 500-800 mm; and 533,000 hectares of farmland were flooded. 257,000 houses, 1,600 bridges, and 1,600 km of roads were destroyed or seriously damaged. (UN DHA, 16 Jul 1986)
A UNDRO/UNDP representative in Beijing reported that a new typhoon and torrential rains struck the coast in Guangxi Zhuang region, leaving 22 dead, 700 injured, and 100 missing. Numerous dikes were broken, hundreds of villages and over 13,300 hectares of farmlands were flooded.  (UN DHA, 25 Jul 1986)
A UNDRO/UNDP representative in Beijing reported heavy floods caused by torrential rains in two northeast provinces of China. Liaoning province: floods killed 30 people, covered 1530 villages, and destroyed 80,000 hectares of crops. More than 250,000 hectares of farmland and thousands of houses were under water. Jilin province: about 2 million people and one million hectares of farmland were reportedly heavily affected by floods. (UN DHA, 6 Aug 1986)
A UNDRO representative reported heavy casualties and property loss caused by Typhoon Vera in Shanghai on Wednesday 27 August 1986. Eight people died, 25 were injured, 500 houses were destroyed, 8 industrial chimneys were blown down, and crops were flooded. (UN DHA, 29 Aug 1986)

name: Bangladesh: Capsized Ferry Boat - May 1986
description: The UNDRO/UNDP resident coordinator reports that a double-decker ferry boat with 1,500 people on board capsized on river Meghna in the southern district of Barisal on the night of Sunday, 25 May 1986. The Last similar disaster was on 20 April 1986. About 600 passengers were feared killed but so far only 157 bodies have been recovered from the river as many victims were swept far downstream. (UN DHA, 28 May 1986)

name: Solomon Islands: Cyclone Namu - May 1986
description:
A UNDRO/UNDP Resident Coordinator reported on the situation following the passage of Cyclone Namu over Solomon Islands on 18 May 1986. The cyclone, which was the worst in five years, struck with wind force between 90 and 120 knots. Widespread flooding and wind damage occurred, particularly in Guadalcanal, Malaita, Makira, Rennel and Bellona. Malaita, the country's most populous island, with 65,000 inhabitants was the worst affected. (UN DHA, 21 May 1986)
The death toll was confirmed as over 100. (UN DHA, 28 May 1986)

name: Sri Lanka: Dam Burst - Apr 1986
description:
On 20 April 1986, an irrigation dam burst at Kantalai reservoir which is located 138 miles northeast of Colombo and covers an area of 110,000 acres. Floodwater washed over dozens of villages. (UN DHA, 23 Apr 1986)
The dam covered an area of 110,000 acres. Floodwater washed over a dozen villages and polluted wells. The UNDRO resident coordinator reports that 14 persons were killed and 25 were missing, 8,000 families were affected and approximately 8,000 persons, mainly children, were living in government camps and were in immediate need of food, clothing, and medicine. (UN DHA, 25 Apr 1986)

name: Bangladesh: Hail Storm/Tornado - Apr 1986
description:
A UNDRO resident representative reported that 100 people were killed and thousands were injured in a severe hailstorm that hit Gopalganj and Pirojpur districts in southern Bangladesh on 14 April. Extensive damage was caused to houses and crops. (UN DHA, 18 Apr 1986)
Following this disaster, a double-decker ferry boat with nearly 1,000 people on board capsized during a storm with 90km/hr winds near Munshiganj on the night of 20 April. At least 165 people were feared drowned and many were missing. (UN DHA, 22 Apr 1986)

name: Fiji/Tonga: Cyclone Martin - Apr 1986
description: Tropical Cyclone Martin hit Vanua Levu and other islands in the northern division during the night on 12/13 April 1986 and passed over central island groups of Tonga during the night on 13/14 April. Winds were over 65 knots and gusts were up to 90 knots with heavy rains. The central division, especially the Suva area, were hard hit by floods and landslides. Rewa river was over 5m above normal. Many roads were washed out or under water and several bridges collapsed. 5,600 persons were reported to be lodged in 42 evacuation centres, and were receiving clothes and rations from EMSEC.(UN DHA, 22 Apr 1986)

name: Myanmar: Fire - Mar 1986
description: Since the serious fires in Kamayut and Thingangyun townships of Rangoon on 4 March 1986, UNDRO has been monitoring the situation. A UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reports that fires destroyed 2,350 houses rendering 20,500 people from 4,300 households homeless. Losses from the Kamayut fire alone estimated at Kyats 60 million, or US dollars 8 million. (UN DHA, 7 Mar 1986)

name: Sri Lanka: Floods and Landslides - Feb 1986
description: Heavy rains in many parts of the country in early January caused floods in eastern and landslides in central provinces. The UNDRO resident representative transmitted the following government figures given on 12/2/86: 43 deaths, 12,897 families affected, 3,030 houses completely destroyed, and 19,250 houses partially damaged. (UN DHA, 17 Feb 1986)

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Oct 1985
description:
Typhoon Dot (Saling) hit southern and central Luzon on 18 and 19 October 1985. The most heavily-affected provinces were Aurora, Nueva Ecija, Tarlac and Bur Lacan. (UN DHA, 22 Oct 1985)
The most recent data provided by the UNDRO resident representative is as follows: 81 dead (corrected figure), 24 injured, 242,023 families affected, and 1,183,602 persons affected. 52,933 houses were destroyed and 200,450 were damaged. (UN DHA, 19 Nov 1985)

name: Typhoon Cecil - Oct 1985
description: Cecil developed as a tropical depression over the Sulu Sea about 250 nautical miles south of Manila on 12 October. It destroyed or sank 70 fishing boats in the Iloilo area of the Philippines. Cecil moved northwestwards over the South China Sea and intensified into a typhoon on 14 October about 210 nautical miles southeast of Xisha. Early on 16 October, it crossed the Viet Nam coast about 80 nautical miles northwest of Danang. Cecil brought heavy damage to central Viet Nam with 769 killed, 128 people missing and about 200 others injured. About 130,000 houses were damaged or collapsed, and 70,000 hectares of crops were devastated. During the evening of 16 October, Cecil moved westwards across Laos into Thailand where it dissipated. Cecil brought heavy rain to northern and northeastern Thailand, and caused the death of one person. ([Government of Hong Kong SAR, 1986] (https://reliefweb.int/node/3410503))

name: China: Floods - Aug 1985
description: A UNDRO resident representative reports a flood disaster in Liaoning province. 200 people were killed, 100 were missing, and more than 1 million hectares of farmland were submerged. More than 10,000 troops were watching day and night on the banks of the Liaohe and emergency measures were taken to contain water in four reservoirs on the upper reaches of the Liaohe River. Most of those whose houses were flooded were evacuated to safe places. (UN DHA, 30 Aug 1985)

name: China: Earthquake - Aug 1985
description: A major earthquake measuring 7.5 on the richter scale occurred near USSR-China border, 325 miles east of Tashkent (USSR), close to Wuqia city (China) about 37 miles northeast Kashgar, at 12.42 GMT, 23 August 1985. 67 deaths and more than 100 injured persons were reported. Remote country villages and towns suffered severe damage. 85 percent of houses were destroyed in Wuqia county. Xinjiang regional government and military rescue teams provided relief. (UN DHA, 26 August 1985)

name: Algeria: Drought Affected Displaced Populations - Mali/Niger - Jul 1985
description: Algeria launched an appeal 9 July 1985 in response to 40,000 drought affected refugees from Mali and Niger.  The displaced settled in the province of Wilaya, southern Algeria. The breakdown of the displaced:  13,097 in Guezzam, 1,947 in Zaouatene, 4,337 in Bordj-Moktar (three border posts). The remaining 20,00 moved towards the town of Tamanrasset and neighbouring areas.OCHA, 10 Jul 1985

name: Philippines: Typhoon - Jun 1985
description:
Typhoon 'Kuring' which hit Luzon and surrounding areas, namely Ilocos Sur, Ilocos Norte, Benguet, Nueva Ecija, Bataan, Zambales, Vigan, Bantay, San Esteban, Laoag, Baguio city, Pampanga, caused deaths and infrastructure damage. The following was reported by the UNDRO resident representative: 11 deaths, over 500 houses were totally destroyed and many were damaged. Over 600 families (3,719 persons) were affected and a total of 194 families (2,901 persons) were homeless. (UN DHA, 26 Jun 1985)
The same regions (Luzon and surrounding areas) were hit by another typhoon, 'Daling' on 30 June 1985. Furthermore, heavy monsoon rains continued to cause flooding and landslides. Over 100 persons are reported as having died and approximately 420,000 persons were affected. Hundreds were made homeless and thousands of homes were partially damaged. (UN DHA, 8 Jul 1985)

name: Bangladesh: Cyclone - May 1985
description:
A severe cyclone struck the coastal belt and offshore islands at midnight on Friday 24 May 1985. Winds were up to 160 mph and there was up to a 3-meter tidal bore. (UN DHA, 26 May 1985)
After a field visit accompanied by a UNDP program officer, and a swiss disaster relief unit representative, a UNDRO delegate reports that the worst affected areas were Coastal Chars Jabbar, Clark, Lakhi and Gang Chil. An in-flight survey of affected Coastal Chars (islands) reveals that the condition of dwelling-houses/shelters was 40 percent total destruction, 30 percent partial destruction, and 30 percent intact. (UN DHA, 7 Jun 1985)
The Government reported that 2,540 bodies had been recovered and estimated that 4,882 were missing. (UN DHA, 11 Jun 1985)

name: Bangladesh: Storm - Apr 1985
description: At least 50 people were killed and thousands were injured when a cyclonic storm hit six villages in Bmarata union of Pabna district in the late afternoon on 28 March 1985. The storm, with winds up to 150 km (95 miles) an hour, demolished 90 percent of the houses, in the six villages. The storm was followed by 30 minutes of an unprecedented hailstorm that caused most of the deaths and injuries and inflicted severe damage to plantations. (UN DHA, 1 Apr 1985)

name: Fiji: Cyclonic Storms (Pacific Region) - Jan 1985
description:
Various reports, including information received from the UNDRO/UNDP resident coordinator (Fiji), inform of serious damage arising from cyclone Eric, having been inflicted on Viti Levu (Fiji). Two further cyclones (Nigel, Odette), heading in the general direction, were taken by cyclone Eric. (UN DHA, 18 Jan 1985)
Eric is out of Fiji areas but hit Tongatapu and Eva islands in Tonga on the 18th and 19th of January. The damage in Tonga is not extensive. Nigel, which is stronger than Eric and still intensifying, is expected to strike Fiji at 1800 hrs on 19 January and is following precisely the same path as Eric. Fiji Meteorological Service stated that Eric would hit Suva. Odette was last reported with winds increasing and is expected off vila in Vanuatu at 0500 hrs, 21 January. Odette was last reported with increasing winds and is expected off Vila in Vanuatu at 0500 hrs on the 21st of January. (UN DHA, 19 Jan 1985)
The UNDRO/UNDP resident representative in Fiji said that cyclone Nigel struck north of Suva on Saturday 19 January at 1900 hrs and lasted three hours. Its strength was reported to be slightly below Eric and its destructive zone was much narrower. (UN DHA, 20 Jan 1985)
In Fiji, the UNDRO/UNDP resident representative reported that the death toll was 28, and the western division was the worst hit with damage to 5,000 to 10,000 houses, school buildings, telecommunications, and water supplies. (UN DHA, 24 Jan 1985)
In Vanuatu, more than 2,000 people were reported as homeless on the islands of Ambae, Maewo, Pentecost, Malekula, and Santo. Food crops were destroyed on all affected islands, and food shortages were expected in the short to medium term. (UN DHA, 24 Jan 1985)
The areas most seriously affected by cyclones Eric and Nigel stretch across the northern islands of Vanuatu (Espiritu Santo, Ambae, the southern part of Maewo, and the northern half of Pentecost).  Only one death resulted from the cyclones and thus medical facilities were considered adequate. (UN DHA, 7 Feb 1985)

description: A tragic gas leakage on 3 December 1984, killed approximately 2,500 people and affected over 150,000 people. Evacuee camps were set up with approximately 70,000 persons needing medical attention, of which 2,000 were hospitalized. Their symptoms include eye, chest, and other health problems. (UN DHA, 27 Dec 1984)

name: Indonesia: Volcanic Eruption - Nov 1984
description:
The UNDRO/UNDP Resident Co-ordinator has reported that Mount Karangetang, situated on Siau island, north Sulawesi, resumed volcanic activity on Thursday, 8 November 1984. Hot vapour and black smoke were emitted. The governor of North Sulawesi reported that villagers within the danger zone were evacuated to safer areas. (UN DHA, 20 Nov 1984)
Further to information report No. 1 of 20 November 1984, the UNDRO/UNDP Resident Co-ordinator has informed that volcano Mt. Karangetang, situated on Siau Island, North Sulawesi, erupted on 21 February 1985 with lava flow reaching 100 metres per hour. A new crater has been created called Dagu Kambing, approximately 100 metres west of the main existing crater. Neighbouring villages were under threat and inhabitants were standing by for a possible full-scale evacuation.  (UN DHA, 1 Mar 1985)

name: India: Cyclone - Nov 1984
description:
A strong cyclonic storm with winds of 100 miles per hour struck the south Indian provinces of Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh on 14-15 Nov 1984. The cyclone was followed by floods inundating large parts of affected provinces. (UN DHA, 20 Nov 1984)
LRCS, quoting its chief liaison officer, currently visiting affected areas, reports that the death toll has risen to 446;  the cyclone destroyed over 100,000 dwellings, uprooted thousands of trees, cut power supply, roads and bridges, and disrupted train services; the worst-hit area was Nellore district in Andhra Pradesh where 20,000 dwellings were destroyed and 316 persons were killed; and nearly 10,000 people are marooned by water. (UN DHA, 23 Nov 1984)

name: Typhoon Agnes - Nov 1984
description:
Typhoon Agnes originated near the Caroline Islands on 1 November. It hit the central Philippines on 5 November, bringing a death toll of at least 727. Several hundred people were reported missing and about 600,000 were made homeless. After crossing the South China Sea, Agnes landed over Viet Nam on 7 November and weakened into an area of low pressure over Thailand on 8 November. (Government of Hong Kong SAR, 1985)
Philippines
Typhoon Undang (Agnes) hit the central regions of Leyte, Negros, Panay and Palawan Islands on 3 November. (UN DHA, 08 November 1984) A final report of casualties and damage from the typhoon stated that there were 862 dead, 197 injured and 217 missing. At least 1,495,738 persons (293,998 families) have been rendered homeless. The total damage to property, crops, livestock, roads and bridges was 1,929.5 million pesos (approx US$96.6 million). (UN DHA, 21 December 1984)
Viet Nam
Typhoon Agnes struck Viet Nam on 9 and 11 November causing damage to the Red River Delta and coastal provinces. Three days of rainfall, varying between 400 and 700mm, caused extensive flooding in Hanoi. (UN DHA, 15 November 1984) Anges caused at least 134 deaths and seriously affected around 650,000 people. At least 31,000 houses were completely destroyed and over 120,000 were flooded and damaged. Out of 198,000 hectares of rice paddies flooded, total crop losses occurred on 55,000 hectares. Crop losses also occurred on 6,310 hectares of maize, 8,652 sweet potatoes and 16,000 manioc. Some 400 schools and 230 nurseries were also destroyed. (UN DHA, 03 December 1984)

name: Mali: Cholera - Oct 1984
description: A cholera epidemic broke out in Jul/Aug 1984 and spread rapidl, approx 850 cases were registered between Koulikoro, Niger and Burkina Faso borders. On 8 Nov,  the Ministery of health and WHO reported that the peak of the epidemic was over. On 26 Oct, 1,475 cases were recorded with 331 deaths. On 31 Oct, 1,614 cases were recorded with 368 deaths. WHO recommended the following actions: oral rehydration, prevention, health education, water and sewage control, especially in North-East, Mopti and Timbouctou. On 8 Aug 1985, the epidemic worsened due to the rainy season therefore new cases were recorded. From 15 May to 15 Jun, 569 cases and114 deaths; from 15 Jun to 15 Jul, 1,031 cases and 172 deaths. WHO reported that between Jan and end of Jul, 2,563 cases with 547 deaths were recorded. (UN DHA, 13 Oct 194)

name: Philippines: Volcanic Eruption - Sep 1984
description:
Volcanic explosions from Mount Mayon in the central Philippines have spewed fiery ash several miles into the sky and sent incandescent rock avalanches streaming down its slopes towards the northwest and southwest. The  Philippine Institute of Volcanology reports ash as falling over a radius of more than 10 miles (15 kms) and tremors have been felt over a wide area. As of 14 September, a total of 16,290 persons were evacuated from Daraga, Legaspi city, Guinobatan, Camalig, Ligao, Tabaco and Sto. Domingo. (UN DHA, 14 Sep 1984)
Seven more eruptions of a strong intensity have been observed. On 16 September, ash-laden clouds ejected to approx 3 kms. Of 29 mild eruptions recorded, 13 were accompanied by ash flows at the southeastern sector of the volcano. Heavy ash flows in several barangays (administrative districts) were situated southwest and northwest of the volcano. Considerable amounts of ash and other pyroclastic deposits have accumulated around Mayon's upper slope. Eighteen evacuation centers have been set up by the provincial disaster coordinating committee in municipalities Camalig, Daraga, Guinobatan, Legaspi city, Ligao and Sto. Domingo. The number of evacuees has now risen to almost 26,000 persons.  (UN DHA, 18 Sep 1984)
After a few days of lull, new explosions on 23 September (the largest since the eruption began on 9 september). The UNDRO/UNDP resident co-ordinator reports as of 25 September: 44,094 persons (7,997 families) are accomodated in 42 evacuation centres.  (UN DHA, 28 Sep 1984)
Damage reported as of 27 September: Legaspi city: 98 percent agricultural crops damaged, water springs destroyed, roads washed out, some houses destroyed. Ministry of social services and development has carried out relief assistance as of 25 september, totalling pesos 1,196,532 (us dlrs 66,474) provided to 41,193 persons (7,252 families). (UN DHA, 28 Sep 1984)

name: Philippines: Typhoons - Sep 1984
description:
Typhoon Maring hit the Philippines on 27 August and was followed by Typhoon Nitang on 1 September. Typhoon Nitang (codename Ike) caused extensive damage over central and southern provinces. The typhoon with gusts of 275 kph (170 mph) at its peak was reported to be the most powerful since 1970. Initial reports transmitted by the UNDRO/UNDP resident co-ordinator indicated that Cebu province was the hardest hit. Provinces Surigao Del Norte and Misamis Oriental in Mindanao were also severely affected. (UN DHA, 3 Sep 1984)
The final report of casualties and damage from typhoon Maring from the UNDRO/UNDP resident coordinator provided the following statistics: 121 confirmed dead; 26 injured; 17 missing; 475,899 persons (92,271 families) were affected; 7,745 persons (1,310 families) were rendered homeless;
and 673 houses were totally destroyed.  (UN DHA, 5 Oct 1984)
According to the final report on casualties and damage from Typhoon Nitang from the UNDRO/UNDP resident co-ordinator: 939 people died; 2,565 were injured; 460 were missing; 1,237,224 persons (203,588 families) were affected; 547,076 persons (91,179 families) were rendered homeless; and 82,140 houses were totally destroyed. (UN DHA, 24 Oct 1984)

name: Indonesia: Volcanic Eruption - Sep 1984
description: UNDRO reported on the building of dykes to divert the flow of volcanic mud from slopes. A UNDRO/UNDP permanent representative reported that a chain of dykes was slowly succumbing to a surge of millions of tons of debris. As a result of persistent rainfall around the peak of a mountain since 29 August, Cikunir, Cikuntan, Cimerah, and Cimampang rivers have swollen bringing volcanic material and breaking dykes. Half of the population of Rancailat village was evacuated as houses were submerged by debris. Damage to farming plots was reported. Approximately 12 hectares of rice fields were covered by volcanic sludge. It is feared that if heavy rains continue, more serious damage could be inflicted on villages along river basins further down the slopes of mt. Galunggung. (UN DHA, 10 Sep 1984)

name: Rep. of Korea: Torrential Rains - Sep 1984
description:
During the period of 31 August to 2 September 1984 torrential rain, with falls of up to 63 cm, hit the central region, Kyunggido, and Kangwondo. Other provinces reportedly affected were Chungchongdo, Kyongsangdo, and Chollado. (UN DHA, 3 Sep 1984)
The UNDRO/UNDP resident coordinator has transmitted the following summary report: 156 dead, 187 injured, and property damage: 131.8 million won (US dollars 167,899). (UN DHA, 19 Sep 1984)

name: Sri Lanka: Monsoon Floods - Jul 1984
description: The UNDRO/UNDP resident representative in Colombo reports that excessive monsoon rains have caused floods in the districts of Ratnapura, Kegalce, Gampaka, and Colombo. Preliminary information indicates 3 deaths, 14,000 families homeless, and over 1,500 houses destroyed. (UN DHA, 14 Jul 1984)

name: Indonesia: Mt. Merapi Volcanic Eruption - Jun 1984
description:
Mt. Merapi in central Java started erupting again on 15 June at 24.11 hrs local time and continued until the next morning.  The hot clouds reached approximately 5,000 metres in height. Surrounding forests were burned and several towns (Salatiga, Magelang, Ambarawa and Semarang) and surrounding areas were covered by ash up to 2-2.5 cm in thickness. (UN DHA, 18 Jun 1984)
Mt Merapi is still active with sporadic eruptions. Ash is being carried northward toward Boyoiali and Semarang. An estimated 39,000 persons are in danger zone one (districts Dukuh, Srumbung and Sawangan) evacuated to safer areas and return to their homes. Approximately 1.5 million cubic metres of lava flowed into Putih river, south-west of Merapi. Mt. Merapi last erupted in 1973, and increasing activity was observed in 1982 and 1983. (UN DHA, 22 Jun 1984)

name: Indonesia: Mt. Soputan Volcanic Eruption - May 1984
description: Mt. Soputan in north Sulawesi province started erupting at 2205h local time on 25 May, spewing columns of smoke with fire more than 3,000 meters high. Scores of houses were destroyed by burning rocks and ash but no casualties were reported. Villages within a radius of 10 kilometers from the volcanic center were declared as danger zones. An estimated 32,000 persons were in 19 villages around the volcano. Mt. Soputan last erupted in 1982, destroying hundreds of farms and forcing people to abandon villages. (UN DHA, 28 May 1984)

name: Bangladesh: Floods - May 1984
description:
Widespread heavy rains from 6-19 May, which was termed unprecedented according to meteorological records, caused severe flooding in eastern Bangladesh. Rain-swollen rivers burst their banks and overflowed into villages and inundated vast areas of agricultural land. Extensive damage to crops and property were reported. Thousands of people were rendered homeless and a greater number suffered heavy losses. The death toll was reported as 91 in Bangladesh. (UN DHA, 25 May 1984)
The most seriously affected areas were Mousavi, Bazar, Sylhet, Sunamganj and Habigunj.(UN DHA, 6 Jun 1984)

description: The Government of Burma has now requested UNDRO to launch an immediate appeal for assistance to the homeless victims of the recent fire. A UNDP/UNDRO res.rep, after a joint UNDP/WHO on-site inspection, reports that emergency relief assistance is still required for 6,900 homeless persons located in relief camps. (UN DHA, 13 Apr 1984)

name: Papua New Guinea: Rabaul Volcano Alert - Apr 1984
description: Since October 1983, the Rabaul volcano in east new Britain province has shown abnormal local earthquakes and ground swelling, typical precursors to eruption. The last eruption, in 1939, was violently destructive causing 500 fatalities. The population now at risk has increased sixfold to 30,000. After a significant increase of eruption precursors, stage 2 of the four-stage alert was declared on 29 October 1983 and extensive preparations were made for stage 3 (full mobilization to evacuate) and stage 4 (general evacuation). Stage 2 could continue for many months without significant changes or could escalate at short notice (within days or hours) to a destructive eruption requiring evacuation. (UN DHA, 27 Apr 1984)

name: Myanmar: Fire - Mar 1984
description: Since a serious fire broke out on 24 March 1984 in Mandalay, UNDRO has been monitoring the situation. A UNDRO/UNDP permanent representative reports over 2,700 houses destroyed and 23,250 persons rendered homeless. Government offices, co-operatives, commercial area, schools, stores, medical centres and cinemas were destroyed. Loss of property is estimated at 33 million US dollars. (UN DHA, 27 Mar 1984)

name: Indonesia: Floods and Landslides - Mar 1984
description: The Indonesian govt national coordination board for natural disaster relief (Bakornas PBA) has advised the UNDRO/UNDP resident representative Jakarta of serious seasonal flooding and landslides in the provinces of central, east, and west Java, Jogyakarta special region, and north Sumatra. Twenty-six persons were reported dead, over 300,000 persons were affected, over 60,000 homes were damaged or destroyed, and approximately 31,000 hectares of rice fields were lost. (UN DHA, 1 Mar 1984)
